what: A Note on Terminology: 2SD, Simulation, and Theory
title: Getting Started with Stat218Applet
text: package uses language ISI textbook directly. three inference methods called 2SD, simulation, theory throughout. correspond : 2SD: simulation-based confidence interval method using rule statistic ± 2 × SD bootstrap distribution. intentionally restricted 95% confidence intervals book introduces concept. confidence levels, use "simulation". simulation: general simulation-based approach (bootstrap CIs, randomization/permutation/sign-flipping tests) works confidence level hypothesis direction. theory: formula-based approach using Z T distributions, appropriate validity conditions met. familiar statistics courses textbooks, “2SD” essentially simplified bootstrap interval, “simulation” full bootstrap randomization test. naming follows ISI students can move textbook package without confusion.

title: Getting Started with Stat218Applet
text: Tests whether single population proportion π equals hypothesized value. Accepts either summary statistics (successes, n) raw data (formula, data, success_level). Uses either simulation (Great Shuffle) theory-based Z-test.
code: # Summary statistics path -- theory method # Testing whether dolphins trained with the "yes" signal respond more than 50% of the time # (from the ISI dolphin study: 15 out of 16 correct responses) result_1prop <- test_1prop(   successes   = 15,   n           = 16,   null_pi     = 0.5,   alternative = "greater",   method      = "theory" ) #> Warning: ! Validity conditions for the theory-based Z-test may not be met. #> ℹ Expected successes under H0: 8 (need >= 10) #> ℹ Expected failures under H0: 8 (need >= 10) #> ℹ Consider using `method = "simulation"` for a more reliable p-value. print(result_1prop) #>  #> ── 1-Sample Proportion Test (Theory) ─────────────────────────────────────────── #> ℹ Sample Proportion (p-hat): 0.9375 (15/16) #> ℹ Null Hypothesis (pi0): 0.5 #> ℹ Alternative: greater #> • SD of Null Distribution: 0.125 #> • Test Statistic (Z): 3.5 #> • P-Value: 2e-04 # Single ggplot -- safe to render plot(result_1prop) # 3-panel patchwork -- run in RStudio plot_steps(result_1prop) data(dolphin) # Raw data path -- simulation method using the bundled dolphin dataset result_1prop_sim <- test_1prop(   formula       = ~ response,   data          = dolphin,   success_level = "Improve",   null_pi       = 0.5,   alternative   = "greater",   method        = "simulation",   sim_reps      = 1000 ) print(result_1prop_sim) # Dotplot is available for simulation -- single ggplot, renders cleanly plot(result_1prop_sim, plot_type = "dotplot")

title: Getting Started with Stat218Applet
text: Tests whether single population mean μ equals hypothesized value. sd_type argument distinguishes known population σ (Z-test) estimated sample s (T-test). Simulation uses parametric bootstrap centered null value.
code: # Raw data path -- theory T-test # Testing whether students underestimate a 10-second interval (null: mu = 10) result_1mean <- test_1mean(   formula     = ~ estimate,   data        = timeestimate,   null_mu     = 10,   alternative = "two.sided",   method      = "theory" ) print(result_1mean) plot(result_1mean) plot_steps(result_1mean) # Summary statistics path -- theory T-test result_1mean_s <- test_1mean(   x_bar       = 9.1,   n           = 48,   sd_val      = 2.4,   sd_type     = "sample",   null_mu     = 10,   alternative = "two.sided",   method      = "theory" ) print(result_1mean_s) #>  #> ── 1-Sample Mean Hypothesis Test (Theory) ────────────────────────────────────── #> ℹ Observed Mean (x-bar): 9.1 #> ℹ Standard Deviation (s): 2.4 | n: 48 #> ℹ Null Hypothesis (mu0): 10 #> ℹ Alternative: two.sided #> • SD of Null Distribution: 0.3464 #> • Test Statistic (T): -2.598 #> • P-Value: 0.0125 # Simulation path -- parametric bootstrap (requires raw data) result_1mean_sim <- test_1mean(   formula     = ~ estimate,   data        = timeestimate,   null_mu     = 10,   alternative = "two.sided",   method      = "simulation",   sim_reps    = 1000 ) print(result_1mean_sim)

title: Getting Started with Stat218Applet
text: Tests whether two population proportions equal (H₀: π₁ − π₂ = 0). Uses either randomization test (simulation) pooled Z-test (theory). plot() method function returns patchwork object – null distribution top formatted 2×2 contingency table bottom – run interactively. Note: plot() test_2prop() ci_2prop() combines null distribution plot gt contingency table using patchwork. Run interactively RStudio see full layout.
code: # Summary statistics path -- theory # Yawning study: does seeing someone yawn make you more likely to yawn? result_2prop <- test_2prop(   success_1   = 10,   n_1         = 34,   success_2   = 4,   n_2         = 16,   group_names = c("Yawn Seed", "No Seed"),   alternative = "greater",   method      = "theory" ) #> Warning: ! Validity conditions for the theory-based Z-test may not be met. #> ℹ All four cells in the 2x2 table must have at least 10 observations. #> ℹ Minimum cell count found: 4 #> ℹ Consider using `method = "simulation"` for a more reliable p-value. print(result_2prop) #>  #> ── 2-Sample Proportions Hypothesis Test (Theory) ─────────────────────────────── #> ℹ Yawn Seed: p1-hat = 0.2941 (successes = 10, n = 34) #> ℹ No Seed: p2-hat = 0.25 (successes = 4, n = 16) #> ℹ Null Hypothesis: pi1 - pi2 = 0 #> ℹ Alternative: pi1 - pi2 > 0 #> • Observed Difference (p1-hat - p2-hat): 0.0441 #> • SD of Null Distribution: 0.1361 #> • Test Statistic (Z): 0.324 #> • P-Value: 0.3729 #> Warning: ! Validity conditions for the theory-based Z-test may not be met. #> ℹ All four cells in the 2x2 table must have at least 10 observations. #> ℹ Minimum cell count found: 4. #> ℹ Consider using `method = "simulation"` for a more reliable result. # Patchwork (null distribution + contingency table) -- run in RStudio plot(result_2prop) plot_steps(result_2prop) # Raw data path -- using the bundled yawning dataset result_2prop_raw <- test_2prop(   formula       = response ~ yawn_seed,   data          = yawning,   success_level = "Yawn",   alternative   = "greater",   method        = "simulation" ) print(result_2prop_raw)

title: Getting Started with Stat218Applet
text: Tests whether two population means equal (H₀: μ₁ − μ₂ = 0). Theory uses unpooled T-test Satterthwaite degrees freedom. Simulation uses permutation test (requires raw data). optional boxplot available raw data provided.
code: # Summary statistics path -- theory T-test # Do men and women pay different amounts for haircuts? result_2mean <- test_2mean(   x_bar_1     = 28.5,   sd_1        = 23.1,   n_1         = 25,   x_bar_2     = 49.2,   sd_2        = 38.4,   n_2         = 25,   group_names = c("Male", "Female"),   alternative = "two.sided",   method      = "theory" ) print(result_2mean) #>  #> ── 2-Sample Means Hypothesis Test (Theory) ───────────────────────────────────── #> ℹ Male: x-bar = 28.5 | s = 23.1 | n = 25 #> ℹ Female: x-bar = 49.2 | s = 38.4 | n = 25 #> ℹ Null Hypothesis: mu1 - mu2 = 0 #> ℹ Alternative: mu1 - mu2 != 0 #> • Observed Difference (x-bar1 - x-bar2): -20.7 #> • SD of Null Distribution: 8.9625 #> • Test Statistic (T): -2.31 #> • P-Value: 0.0262 # Single null distribution plot -- renders cleanly plot(result_2mean) # Raw data path -- using the bundled haircuts dataset result_2mean_raw <- test_2mean(   formula     = cost ~ sex,   data        = haircuts,   alternative = "two.sided",   method      = "theory" ) print(result_2mean_raw) # Boxplot option -- requires raw data, run in RStudio plot(result_2mean_raw, plot_type = "boxplot") plot_steps(result_2mean_raw)

title: Getting Started with Stat218Applet
text: Tests whether mean paired differences equals zero (H₀: μ_d = 0). Accepts three input formats: summary statistics (x_bar_d, sd_d, n_d), single differences column (~ Differences), two columns representing /measurements (~ ). Simulation uses sign-flipping approach requires raw data.
code: # Two-column formula: wide - narrow for each baseball player result_paired <- test_paired(   formula     = wide ~ narrow,   data        = firstbase,   name        = "Wide - Narrow",   alternative = "two.sided",   method      = "theory" ) print(result_paired) plot(result_paired) plot_steps(result_paired) # Summary statistics path result_paired_s <- test_paired(   x_bar_d     = 0.075,   sd_d        = 0.06,   n_d         = 22,   name        = "Wide - Narrow",   alternative = "two.sided",   method      = "theory" ) print(result_paired_s) #>  #> ── Paired Data Hypothesis Test (Theory) ──────────────────────────────────────── #> ℹ Mean Difference (x-bar_d): 0.075 #> ℹ SD of Differences (s_d): 0.06 #> ℹ Number of Pairs (n_d): 22 #> ℹ Null Hypothesis: mu_d = 0 #> ℹ Alternative: mu_d != 0 #> • SD of Null Distribution: 0.0128 #> • Test Statistic (T): 5.863 #> • P-Value: 0 # Sign-flipping simulation -- requires raw data result_paired_sim <- test_paired(   formula     = bicycle ~ jj,   data        = jjvsbicycle,   name        = "Bicycle - JJ",   alternative = "two.sided",   method      = "simulation",   sim_reps    = 1000 ) print(result_paired_sim)

title: Getting Started with Stat218Applet
text: Tests whether population correlation ρ equals zero (H₀: ρ = 0). Accepts either observed correlation sample size directly (r_obs, n) raw data via formula. Theory converts r T-statistic df = n − 2. Simulation shuffles response variable repeatedly.
code: # Raw data path -- theory result_corr <- test_correlation(   formula     = perceived_weight ~ hand_width,   data        = handwidth,   alternative = "two.sided",   method      = "theory" ) print(result_corr) plot(result_corr) plot_steps(result_corr) # Summary statistics path result_corr_s <- test_correlation(   r_obs       = 0.72,   n           = 46,   alternative = "two.sided",   method      = "theory" ) print(result_corr_s)

title: Getting Started with Stat218Applet
text: Tests whether population slope β₁ equals zero (H₀: β₁ = 0). Accepts slope, standard error, sample size directly (slope, se, n) fits model raw data via formula. Theory uses T-statistic df = n − 2. Simulation shuffles response variable break relationship.
code: # Raw data path -- theory result_reg <- test_regression(   formula     = score ~ time,   data        = examtimesscores,   alternative = "two.sided",   method      = "theory" ) print(result_reg) plot(result_reg) plot_steps(result_reg) # Summary statistics path result_reg_s <- test_regression(   slope       = -0.32,   se          = 0.18,   n           = 30,   alternative = "two.sided",   method      = "theory" ) print(result_reg_s)

title: Getting Started with Stat218Applet
text: running formal inference, helps explore data understand regression output. three helper functions designed support process.

title: Getting Started with Stat218Applet
text: function automatically detects variable types formula routes appropriate visualization. arguments beyond formula data required default behavior. Two numeric variables → scatterplot crosshair lines correlation annotation (default), regression line equation panel (fit_line = TRUE). Numeric response, categorical explanatory → side--side boxplots individual data points, group means (red triangle), group sample sizes. Always run explore_2vars() formal test. helps check whether relationship looks linear (correlation/regression) whether group distributions look similar (two-mean tests).
code: # Two numeric variables -- correlation mode explore_2vars(   formula = perceived_weight ~ hand_width,   data    = handwidth ) # Regression mode -- patchwork (scatterplot + equation panel), run in RStudio explore_2vars(   formula  = size ~ year,   data     = platesize,   fit_line = TRUE ) # Numeric ~ categorical -- side-by-side boxplots explore_2vars(   formula = cost ~ sex,   data    = haircuts )

title: Getting Started with Stat218Applet
text: Fits simple linear regression model produces formatted gt table. Two table types available: table_type = "regression" (default): Shows intercept slope standard errors, T-statistics, p-values, confidence interval. Includes plain-English interpretation slope CI footer. table_type = "anova": Shows ANOVA decomposition – Model, Error, Total rows sums squares, degrees freedom, mean squares, F-statistic, R². Reading ANOVA table: Model row tells much total variability response explained regression line. Error row ’s left . R² = SS_Model / SS_Total, shown footer. simple linear regression, F-test ANOVA table tests exact null hypothesis slope T-test – H₀: β₁ = 0 – always find F = T².
code: # Regression coefficients table table_regression(   formula    = size ~ year,   data       = platesize,   table_type = "regression" ) # ANOVA decomposition table table_regression(   formula    = size ~ year,   data       = platesize,   table_type = "anova" )

title: Getting Started with Stat218Applet
text: five confidence interval functions share three-method structure: "2SD" (default, 95% ), "simulation" (confidence level), "theory" (formula-based). printed output, plot, plot_steps methods follow structure hypothesis test functions.

title: Getting Started with Stat218Applet
text: Constructs confidence interval single population proportion π. default "2SD" method generates bootstrap distribution centered p̂ uses ±2 × SD rule. plot() method includes distribution shaded CI region forest plot – single ggplot.
code: # 2SD method (default) -- summary statistics result_ci1p <- ci_1prop(successes = 15, n = 16) print(result_ci1p) plot(result_ci1p) # Theory method result_ci1p_t <- ci_1prop(   successes  = 15,   n          = 16,   conf_level = 0.95,   method     = "theory" ) #> Warning: ! Validity conditions for the theory-based interval may not be met. #> ℹ Observed successes: 15 (need ≥ 10) #> ℹ Observed failures: 1 (need ≥ 10) #> ℹ Consider using `method = "2SD"` or `method = "simulation"` instead. print(result_ci1p_t) #>  #> ── 95% Confidence Interval (Theory) ──────────────────────────────────────────── #> ℹ Point Estimate (p-hat): 0.9375 (15 successes out of n = 16) #> ℹ SD of Sampling Distribution: 0.0605 #> • Interval: (0.8189, 1.0561) #> Warning: ! Validity conditions may not be met. #> ℹ Observed successes: 15 | Observed failures: 1 (both need ≥ 10). #> ℹ Consider using `method = "2SD"` or `method = "simulation"`. # Raw data -- simulation method, 90% CI result_ci1p_raw <- ci_1prop(   formula       = ~ response,   data          = dolphin,   success_level = "Improve",   conf_level    = 0.90,   method        = "simulation" ) print(result_ci1p_raw) # Dotplot for simulation methods -- run in RStudio plot(result_ci1p_raw, plot_type = "dotplot") plot_steps(result_ci1p)

title: Getting Started with Stat218Applet
text: Constructs confidence interval single population mean μ. sd_type = "sample" (default) method = "theory", T-multiplier used. sd_type = "population", Z-multiplier used.
code: # 2SD method (default) -- raw data result_ci1m <- ci_1mean(   formula = ~ estimate,   data    = timeestimate ) print(result_ci1m) plot(result_ci1m) # Theory method -- summary statistics, 99% CI result_ci1m_t <- ci_1mean(   x_bar      = 9.1,   n          = 48,   sd_val     = 2.4,   sd_type    = "sample",   conf_level = 0.99,   method     = "theory" ) print(result_ci1m_t) #>  #> ── 99% Confidence Interval (Theory) ──────────────────────────────────────────── #> ℹ Point Estimate (x-bar): 9.1 #> ℹ Standard Deviation (s): 2.4 | n: 48 #> ℹ SD of Sampling Distribution: 0.3464 #> • Interval: (8.17, 10.03) plot_steps(result_ci1m)

title: Getting Started with Stat218Applet
text: Constructs confidence interval difference two population proportions (π₁ − π₂). bootstrap independently resamples group. theory method uses unpooled standard error (unlike hypothesis test, pools). plot() method combines bootstrap/theory distribution formatted contingency table using patchwork.
code: # Summary statistics -- theory method result_ci2p <- ci_2prop(   success_1   = 10,   n_1         = 34,   success_2   = 4,   n_2         = 16,   group_names = c("Yawn Seed", "No Seed"),   method      = "theory" ) #> Warning: ! Validity conditions for the theory-based interval may not be met. #> ℹ All four cells in the 2x2 table must have at least 10 observations. #> ℹ Minimum cell count found: 4. #> ℹ Consider using `method = "2SD"` or `method = "simulation"` instead. print(result_ci2p) #>  #> ── 95% Confidence Interval for Difference in Proportions (Theory) ────────────── #> ℹ Yawn Seed: p-hat1 = 0.2941 (successes = 10, n = 34) #> ℹ No Seed: p-hat2 = 0.25 (successes = 4, n = 16) #> ℹ Point Estimate (p-hat1 - p-hat2): 0.0441 #> ℹ SD of Sampling Distribution: 0.1335 #> • Interval: (-0.2176, 0.3058) #> Warning: ! Validity conditions may not be met -- at least one cell has fewer than 10 #>   observations. #> ℹ Minimum cell count: 4. #> ℹ Consider using `method = "2SD"` or `method = "simulation"`. # Raw data -- 2SD method result_ci2p_raw <- ci_2prop(   formula       = response ~ yawn_seed,   data          = yawning,   success_level = "Yawn",   method        = "2SD" ) print(result_ci2p_raw) # Patchwork (CI distribution + contingency table) -- run in RStudio plot(result_ci2p) plot_steps(result_ci2p)

title: Getting Started with Stat218Applet
text: Constructs confidence interval difference two population means (μ₁ − μ₂). bootstrap generates separate parametric bootstrap samples group. Theory uses Satterthwaite T-multiplier. Simulation requires raw data.
code: # Summary statistics -- theory method result_ci2m <- ci_2mean(   x_bar_1     = 28.5,   sd_1        = 23.1,   n_1         = 25,   x_bar_2     = 49.2,   sd_2        = 38.4,   n_2         = 25,   group_names = c("Male", "Female"),   method      = "theory" ) print(result_ci2m) plot(result_ci2m) # Raw data -- 2SD method result_ci2m_raw <- ci_2mean(   formula = cost ~ sex,   data    = haircuts,   method  = "2SD" ) print(result_ci2m_raw) # Boxplot -- requires raw data, run in RStudio plot(result_ci2m_raw, plot_type = "boxplot") plot_steps(result_ci2m_raw)

title: Getting Started with Stat218Applet
text: Constructs confidence interval mean paired differences μ_d. Accepts three input formats test_paired(): summary statistics, single differences column, two /columns. sign-flipping bootstrap centered observed x̄_d rather zero.
code: # Two-column formula -- theory method result_cip <- ci_paired(   formula    = wide ~ narrow,   data       = firstbase,   name       = "Wide - Narrow",   method     = "theory" ) print(result_cip) plot(result_cip) # Summary statistics -- theory, 90% CI result_cip_s <- ci_paired(   x_bar_d    = 0.075,   sd_d       = 0.06,   n_d        = 22,   name       = "Wide - Narrow",   conf_level = 0.90,   method     = "theory" ) print(result_cip_s) #>  #> ── 90% Confidence Interval for Mean Difference (Theory) ──────────────────────── #> ℹ Point Estimate (x-bar_d): 0.075 #> ℹ SD of Differences (s_d): 0.06 #> ℹ Number of Pairs (n_d): 22 #> ℹ SD of Sampling Distribution: 0.0128 #> • Interval: (0.053, 0.097) # Two-column simulation -- sign-flipping bootstrap result_cip_sim <- ci_paired(   formula    = bicycle ~ jj,   data       = jjvsbicycle,   name       = "Bicycle - JJ",   conf_level = 0.95,   method     = "simulation" ) print(result_cip_sim) plot_steps(result_cip)

what: Explore the Relationship Between Two Variables — explore_2vars
title: Explore the Relationship Between Two Variables — explore_2vars
text: Produces appropriate visualization summary statistics relationship two variables. function automatically detects variable types routes correct display mode: numeric – Scatterplot crosshairs correlation annotation (correlation mode), regression line equation panel (regression mode). optional group argument produces separate colored points lines per group. Numeric response, categorical explanatory – Side--side boxplots individual points overlaid, group means marked, group sample sizes annotated. Use running test_2mean() ci_2mean(). function accepts input via formula data .

title: Explore the Relationship Between Two Variables — explore_2vars
code: explore_2vars(formula, data, group = NULL, fit_line = FALSE)

title: Explore the Relationship Between Two Variables — explore_2vars
text: formula two-sided formula form response ~ explanatory. response variable (y) placed vertical axis explanatory variable (x) horizontal axis. quantitative categorical explanatory variables supported. example: formula = gpa ~ hours (numeric – scatterplot) formula = time ~ frame (numeric ~ categorical – boxplot) data data frame containing variables named formula , optionally, variable named group. group optional character string naming categorical column data use grouping. applies variables numeric. supplied, separate colored points, crosshairs (correlation mode), regression lines (regression mode) drawn group level. example, group = "Sex" data column called Sex. Default NULL (grouping). fit_line Logical. applies variables numeric. Controls display mode used: FALSE (default) Correlation Mode – draws crosshairs sample means annotates correlation coefficient. Use first assess form, direction, strength association running test_correlation(). TRUE Regression Mode – overlays least-squares regression line displays fitted equation \(R^2\) second panel. Use running test_regression(). explanatory variable categorical, fit_line ignored boxplot always produced.

title: Explore the Relationship Between Two Variables — explore_2vars
text: ggplot2 object (correlation boxplot mode) patchwork object combining scatterplot equation panel (regression mode). result can customized standard ggplot2 functions needed.

title: Explore the Relationship Between Two Variables — explore_2vars
text: Always explore data visually running formal inference procedure. explore_2vars() designed natural first step: test_2mean() ci_2mean(): use explore_2vars(formula = response ~ group, data = mydata) see side--side boxplots two groups. test_correlation(): use explore_2vars(formula = y ~ x, data = mydata) check linear relationship. test_regression(): use explore_2vars(formula = y ~ x, data = mydata, fit_line = TRUE) see regression line equation.

title: Explore the Relationship Between Two Variables — explore_2vars
text: explanatory variable categorical, function produces: Side--side boxplots group Individual data points overlaid (jittered avoid overlap) red triangle marking group mean Group sample sizes annotated box subtitle summarizing mean SD group

title: Explore the Relationship Between Two Variables — explore_2vars
text: correlation mode, subtitle automatically interprets \(r\): \(|r| \geq 0.7\): Strong \(0.4 \leq |r| < 0.7\): Moderate \(|r| < 0.4\): Weak Direction described Positive (\(r > 0\)) Negative (\(r < 0\)).

title: Explore the Relationship Between Two Variables — explore_2vars
text: regression mode, lower panel displays: general form: \(\hat{y} = b_0 + b_1 x\) specific fitted equation numeric coefficients correlation coefficient \(r\) \(R^2\) group supplied, separate equations shown group.

title: Explore the Relationship Between Two Variables — explore_2vars
code: # --- Boxplot Mode: numeric response, categorical explanatory --- # Do carbon and steel bike frames produce different ride times? explore_2vars(   formula = time ~ frame,   data    = biketimes )   # Do breastfed children have higher GCI scores? explore_2vars(   formula = gci ~ feeding,   data    = breastfeedintell )   # --- Correlation Mode: both numeric --- # Is there a linear association between body temp and heart rate? explore_2vars(   formula = heart_rate ~ body_temp,   data    = tempheart )   # --- Regression Mode: both numeric --- # How well does study time predict GPA? explore_2vars(   formula  = gpa ~ hours,   data     = gpa,   fit_line = TRUE ) #> `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'   # --- Regression Mode with Grouping --- if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ mtcars$engine_type <- ifelse(mtcars$vs == 0, "V-Shaped", "Straight") explore_2vars(   formula  = mpg ~ wt,   data     = mtcars,   group    = "engine_type",   fit_line = TRUE ) } # }

what: Show Statistical Symbol Reference Plots — show_symbols

text: Renders one educational reference plots display statistical symbols used throughout STAT 218, organized topic. Symbols presented two-column layout contrasting Population (Greek/Parameter) symbols left Sample (Latin/Statistic) symbols right. called arguments, five plots rendered sequentially RStudio plot viewer — use left/right arrow buttons Plot tab scroll .

code: show_symbols(topic = "all")

title: Show Statistical Symbol Reference Plots — show_symbols
text: topic character string specifying symbol plot display. Must one : "" Renders five plots sequence (default). "proportions" Symbols related proportions (pi, p-hat, etc.). "means" Symbols related means quantitative variables. "tests" Test statistics, decision-making symbols (Z, T, alpha, p-value). "regression" Symbols correlation simple linear regression. "mistakes" Common symbol mistakes students make STAT 218.

title: Show Statistical Symbol Reference Plots — show_symbols
text: Invisibly returns NULL. function called side effect rendering plots active graphics device.

what: The Core Idea: Greek vs. Latin
title: Show Statistical Symbol Reference Plots — show_symbols
text: statistics, Greek letters (mu, sigma, pi, rho, beta) refer population parameters — true values usually unknown. Latin (English) letters (x-bar, s, p-hat, r, b) refer sample statistics — values actually calculate data.

title: Show Statistical Symbol Reference Plots — show_symbols
text: Proportions — categorical data, success/failure outcomes Means — quantitative data, averages spread Test Statistics — standardized statistics decision rules Regression — linear relationships two quantitative variables Common Mistakes — frequent symbol confusions avoid

title: Show Statistical Symbol Reference Plots — show_symbols
code: # Show all symbol plots (scroll with arrows in RStudio Plot tab) show_symbols()       # Show only the proportions reference show_symbols("proportions")   # Show only the means reference show_symbols("means")   # Show the common mistakes plot show_symbols("mistakes")

what: Formatted Simple Linear Regression and ANOVA Tables — table_regression
title: Formatted Simple Linear Regression and ANOVA Tables — table_regression
text: Fits simple linear regression model produces publication-quality formatted table using gt package. Two table types available: Regression Table (table_type = "regression", default): Displays intercept slope standard errors, t-statistics, p-values, confidence interval slope. Includes automatically generated plain-English interpretation confidence interval. ANOVA Table (table_type = "anova"): Displays analysis variance decomposition regression model – Model (Regression), Error (Residuals), Total rows – sums squares, degrees freedom, mean squares, F-statistic, p-value. Includes \(R^2\) footer. function accepts input via formula data – raw data required fit model.

title: Formatted Simple Linear Regression and ANOVA Tables — table_regression
code: table_regression(formula, data, table_type = "regression", conf_level = 0.95)

title: Formatted Simple Linear Regression and ANOVA Tables — table_regression
text: formula two-sided formula form response ~ explanatory identifying response (y) explanatory (x) variables dataset. example, formula = mpg ~ wt columns exist data. data data frame containing variables named formula. table_type character string controlling table produced. Must one : "regression" (default) Produces coefficients table intercept slope, standard errors, t-statistics, p-values, confidence interval slope. plain-English interpretation slope confidence interval shown table footer. "anova" Produces ANOVA decomposition table showing Model (Regression), Error (Residuals), Total rows sums squares, degrees freedom, mean squares, F-statistic, p-value. \(R^2\) shown table footer. conf_level confidence level slope confidence interval shown regression table. Must number strictly 0 Default 0.95 (95% confidence interval). used table_type = "regression".

title: Formatted Simple Linear Regression and ANOVA Tables — table_regression
text: gt table object. table can customized using standard gt functions, saved HTML, PDF, Word using gt::gtsave().

title: Formatted Simple Linear Regression and ANOVA Tables — table_regression
text: regression table displays fitted model $$\hat{y} = b_0 + b_1 x$$ following columns: Term: Intercept (\(b_0\)) explanatory variable name (\(b_1\)) Coefficient: estimated value term SE: Standard error estimate t-stat: standardized test statistic (\(t = b_1 / SE_{b_1}\)) p-value: Two-sided p-value testing H\(_0\): \(\beta = 0\) Lower / Upper: Confidence interval bounds term footer provides plain-English interpretation slope confidence interval, automatically adjusting wording based whether interval entirely positive, entirely negative, straddles zero.

title: Formatted Simple Linear Regression and ANOVA Tables — table_regression
text: ANOVA table decomposes total variability response variable two sources: Model (Regression): Variability explained linear relationship explanatory variable (\(SS_{Model}\), \(df = 1\)) Error (Residuals): Variability left unexplained model (\(SS_{Error}\), \(df = n - 2\)) Total: Total variability response (\(SS_{Total}\), \(df = n - 1\)) F-statistic \(F = MS_{Model} / MS_{Error}\) tests null hypothesis slope t-test: H\(_0\): \(\beta_1 = 0\). \(R^2 = SS_{Model} / SS_{Total}\) shown footer.

title: Formatted Simple Linear Regression and ANOVA Tables — table_regression
text: simple linear regression one explanatory variable, F-test ANOVA table two-sided t-test slope regression table mathematically equivalent: \(F = t^2\) produce p-value.

title: Formatted Simple Linear Regression and ANOVA Tables — table_regression
code: # --- Regression table, mtcars --- # Does car weight predict fuel efficiency? table_regression(   formula = mpg ~ wt,   data    = mtcars )     Simple Linear Regression Output     Model: mpg ~ wt     Term       Coefficient       Std. Error       t-statistic       p-value       Lower 95%       Upper 95%     Intercept 37.2851 1.8776 19.8576 <1e-04 33.4505 41.1198wt −5.3445 0.5591 −9.5590 <1e-04 −6.4863 −4.2026CI Interpretation: We are 95% confident that for every 1 unit increase in wt, the true population mean mpg will decrease between 4.2026 and 6.4863 units.      # --- Regression table, custom confidence level --- table_regression(   formula    = mpg ~ wt,   data       = mtcars,   conf_level = 0.99 )     Simple Linear Regression Output     Model: mpg ~ wt     Term       Coefficient       Std. Error       t-statistic       p-value       Lower 99%       Upper 99%     Intercept 37.2851 1.8776 19.8576 <1e-04 32.1217 42.4486wt −5.3445 0.5591 −9.5590 <1e-04 −6.8820 −3.8069CI Interpretation: We are 99% confident that for every 1 unit increase in wt, the true population mean mpg will decrease between 3.8069 and 6.882 units.      # --- ANOVA table, mtcars --- table_regression(   formula    = mpg ~ wt,   data       = mtcars,   table_type = "anova" )     ANOVA Table: Simple Linear Regression     Model: mpg ~ wt     Source       Sum of Squares       df       Mean Square       F-statistic       p-value     Model (Regression) 847.7252 1 847.7252 91.3753 <1e-04Error (Residuals) 278.3219 30 9.2774 – –Total 1,126.0472 31 – – –R² = 0.7528 – The linear relationship with wt explains 75.28% of the variability in mpg.      # --- Regression table, ISI dataset --- # Does study time predict GPA? table_regression(   formula = gpa ~ hours,   data    = gpa )     Simple Linear Regression Output     Model: gpa ~ hours     Term       Coefficient       Std. Error       t-statistic       p-value       Lower 95%       Upper 95%     Intercept 3.5977 0.1857 19.3702 <1e-04 3.2223 3.9731hours −0.0059 0.0031 −1.9166 0.0624 −0.0121 0.0003CI Interpretation: We are 95% confident that for every 1 unit increase in hours, the true population mean gpa will change by between -0.0121 and 3e-04 units.      # --- ANOVA table, ISI dataset --- table_regression(   formula    = gpa ~ hours,   data       = gpa,   table_type = "anova" )     ANOVA Table: Simple Linear Regression     Model: gpa ~ hours     Source       Sum of Squares       df       Mean Square       F-statistic       p-value     Model (Regression) 0.6803 1 0.6803 3.6735 0.0624Error (Residuals) 7.4079 40 0.1852 – –Total 8.0882 41 – – –R² = 0.0841 – The linear relationship with hours explains 8.41% of the variability in gpa.      # --- Regression table, iris dataset --- # Does sepal length predict petal length? table_regression(   formula = Petal.Length ~ Sepal.Length,   data    = iris )     Simple Linear Regression Output     Model: Petal.Length ~ Sepal.Length     Term       Coefficient       Std. Error       t-statistic       p-value       Lower 95%       Upper 95%     Intercept −7.1014 0.5067 −14.0161 <1e-04 −8.1027 −6.1002Sepal.Length 1.8584 0.0859 21.6460 <1e-04 1.6888 2.0281CI Interpretation: We are 95% confident that for every 1 unit increase in Sepal.Length, the true population mean Petal.Length will increase between 1.6888 and 2.0281 units.      # --- ANOVA table, iris dataset --- table_regression(   formula    = Petal.Length ~ Sepal.Length,   data       = iris,   table_type = "anova" )     ANOVA Table: Simple Linear Regression     Model: Petal.Length ~ Sepal.Length     Source       Sum of Squares       df       Mean Square       F-statistic       p-value     Model (Regression) 352.8662 1 352.8662 468.5502 <1e-04Error (Residuals) 111.4592 148 0.7531 – –Total 464.3254 149 – – –R² = 0.76 – The linear relationship with Sepal.Length explains 76% of the variability in Petal.Length.
